text:
GENITOURINARY MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP VISIT

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Attending Oncologist]
PATIENT: Gerald Hutchinson
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Follow-up for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), 3 months after initiation of Xtandi.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Hutchinson is a 70-year-old male with a known history of high-risk locally advanced prostatic adenocarcinoma (Gleason 4+5=9, Grade Group 5, cT3bN1M0 at diagnosis, confirmed germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant) who presents today for his 3-month follow-up on enza.

By way of background: the patient was initially diagnosed approximately three years ago with bilateral bulky prostate cancer with seminal vesicle invasion and right obturator/iliac chain lymph node involvement. He was treated with combined androgen deprivation therapy (leup acetate) plus Zytiga acetate with prednisone, followed by definitive IMRT/IGRT to the prostate and pelvis (78 Gy in 39 fractions to the prostate/seminal vesicles, with pelvic nodal coverage). He had an excellent initial biochemical response, with a PSA nadir of 0.4 ng/mL at 6 months.

Approximately 9 months ago (at 36 months from initial systemic therapy), he developed biochemical castration-resistant prostate cancer with a PSA doubling time of roughly 3.5 months -- PSA rising through 0.3, 0.6, 1.1, and then 2.3 ng/mL on sequential assessments, with castrate testosterone confirmed at 9 ng/dL. abi was discontinued at that time. Germline testing (Myriad myRisk panel) confirmed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift variant (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22). Somatic tumor profiling (Foundation One CDx, re-biopsy of the prostate at CRPC declaration) confirmed biallelic BRCA2 loss with LOH, along with AR amplification (8x), TP53 p.R175H, RB1 deletion, PTEN homozygous deletion, MYC amplification, elevated gLOH at 18%, MSS, and low TMB. Notably, no AR point mutations (T878A or F877L) were detected.

Conventional restaging imaging at CRPC declaration showed no definitive metastatic disease on CT and bone scan. However, a 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT revealed PSMA-avid adenopathy in the right iliac chain (1.1 cm, SUVmax 4.2) and a focus of uptake at the right posterior iliac crest (SUVmax 3.8) concerning for an early osseous metastasis not seen on bone scan, thereby upstaging the patient to metastatic CRPC by PET criteria.

Given the NGS data showing AR amplification without AR point mutations, and after multidisciplinary discussion, enza 160 mg orally once daily was initiated approximately 3 months ago. Lupron is being continued for testosterone suppression. The rationale was to pursue next-generation AR signaling inhibition before pivoting to Lynparza (given the BRCA2 biallelic loss), in part because his disease burden at CRPC declaration was quite limited by PET and essentially undetectable by conventional imaging.

Today, Mr. Hutchinson presents for his first formal on-treatment assessment. He reports that overall he is getting along reasonably well. He notes a low-grade fatigue that has been persistent since starting Xtandi -- he rates it as a 3/10, mild, but noticeable compared to his baseline. He also describes what he calls "brain fog" -- some word-finding difficulty, occasional difficulty concentrating during conversations, and what his wife describes as seeming a bit "slower" in his responses. He finds this bothersome but not disabling. He is still managing his own medications, finances, and driving independently. He denies any falls, near-falls, or episodes of loss of balance. He explicitly denies any seizure-like activity. No new focal neurological deficits.

He denies any new or worsening bone pain. The low-level pelvic discomfort he had intermittently prior to starting Xtandi has resolved. No urinary retention or hematuria. Appetite is fair; mild early satiety. No significant weight change noted by the patient. Bowel function is regular.

He continues on leup 22.5 mg IM every 3 months, enza 160 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily for hypertension, metformin 1000 mg twice daily for type 2 diabetes, aspirin 81 mg daily, and calcium/vitamin D supplementation. He is taking ondansetron occasionally for nausea but reports this has not been a major issue. He denies taking any new supplements or herbal remedies and was counseled at the last visit regarding drug-drug interactions with enza.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive for: fatigue (grade 1, chronic), mild cognitive fogginess/word-finding difficulty, hot flashes (chronic, grade 1-2, stable on ADT), intermittent nocturia x1-2 per night (residual post-radiation changes, stable), mild decreased libido (expected on ADT).

Negative for: fever, chills, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, new or worsening bone pain, headache, visual changes, diplopia, focal weakness, numbness or tingling, dyspnea, chest pain, palpitations, hemoptysis, hematuria, hematochezia, melena, dysphagia, significant nausea/vomiting, fall or seizure.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/74 mmHg, heart rate 66 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 14, temperature 98.2°F, SpO2 97% on room air, weight 182 lbs (stable from prior visit).

General: Well-appearing male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Speech fluent with mild latency noted during history-taking; wife confirms this is new since starting Xtandi.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal and reactive. No scleral icterus.

Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

Chest/Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No lower extremity edema.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated. No palpable masses.

Musculoskeletal: No tenderness to palpation over the spine, pelvis, or ribs. Full range of motion of all extremities. Gait is steady and normal.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Strength 5/5 throughout. Sensation grossly intact. Coordination normal. Romberg negative. No tremor.

Genitourinary: Digital rectal exam deferred (patient declines, last DRE at prior visit showed no palpable nodularity in the prostate bed).

Lymphadenopathy: No inguinal, axillary, or palpable peripheral adenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

PSA: 0.9 ng/mL -- this represents a 61% decline from the baseline value of 2.3 ng/mL at Xtandi initiation. Notably, prior nadir on ADT plus abi was 0.2-0.4 ng/mL, and this current value reflects the PSA trajectory during early treatment response.

Testosterone: 10 ng/dL -- confirmed castrate (below 50 ng/dL threshold).

CBC: Hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL, WBC 5.8 x10^3/mcL, platelets 204,000/mcL. Mild persistent anemia consistent with chronic disease and ADT effects.

Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.1 mEq/L, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR 68 mL/min/1.73m^2, stable), BUN 18 mg/dL. AST 28 U/L, ALT 31 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 94 U/L (within normal limits; note: ALP elevation would prompt concern for bone disease progression). Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL. Albumin 3.9 g/dL.

HbA1c: 7.1% (adequate glycemic control on metformin; diabetes management per primary care).

Lipid panel: LDL 98 mg/dL. No new dyslipidemia requiring intervention.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

No new imaging obtained at this visit. Prior restaging PSMA PET/CT (obtained at time of CRPC declaration, approximately 3 months ago) showed limited metastatic disease: PSMA-avid right iliac chain adenopathy (1.1 cm, SUVmax 4.2) and right posterior iliac crest osseous focus (SUVmax 3.8), consistent with low-volume mCRPC by PET criteria. No visceral metastases were identified at that time. Repeat imaging is scheduled at the 6-month mark per plan.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. METASTATIC CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER (mCRPC), BRCA2-MUTATED (GERMLINE + SOMATIC BIALLELIC LOSS) -- CURRENTLY ON Xtandi

Mr. Hutchinson is demonstrating a meaningful biochemical response to Xtandi at 3 months, with a PSA decline of 61% (from 2.3 to 0.9 ng/mL). This is an encouraging early signal, consistent with a partial PSA response, though the degree of response is somewhat attenuated relative to what one might expect with a first ARSI -- which is not surprising given that he had already progressed through approximately 3 years of abi. AR amplification on his tumor NGS without AR-LBD point mutations supports some residual AR dependence, which may be driving this response.

I have discussed with Mr. Hutchinson and his wife that while this PSA response is favorable, we should maintain appropriate expectations: prior abi exposure, AR amplification, and the aggressive genomic co-alterations in his tumor (TP53, RB1 loss, PTEN loss) all portend a moderate duration of response at best. We will reassess with imaging at the 6-month mark.

Plan:
- Continue enza 160 mg orally once daily. No dose modification at this time.
- Continue leup acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months for testosterone suppression (next dose due in approximately 6 weeks -- will coordinate with urology/nurse).
- PSA and testosterone to be checked in 6 weeks (mid-cycle check) and again at 6-month restaging visit.
- Restaging imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and whole-body bone scan) at approximately 6 months from Xtandi initiation.

2. enza-RELATED NEUROCOGNITIVE TOXICITY (COGNITIVE FOGGINESS, GRADE 1)

Mr. Hutchinson's cognitive complaints -- word-finding difficulty, slowed processing speed -- are consistent with the known CNS adverse effects of Xtandi, which crosses the blood-brain barrier more than earlier anti-androgens. This is a well-recognized class effect seen in a meaningful proportion of patients, particularly older men. Given that this is grade 1 and he remains functionally independent, dose reduction is not indicated at this time.

Plan:
- Patient counseled to avoid activities requiring full cognitive acuity if impaired (e.g., driving long distances, complex financial decisions during cognitive dips).
- Referred to occupational therapy for cognitive support strategies and baseline neuropsychological screening if symptoms worsen.
- If grade 2 or higher neurocognitive toxicity emerges, will reassess the risk/benefit of continuing enza at full dose.
- Fall precautions reviewed with patient and wife. Patient denies falls or near-falls and this is re-emphasized as a safety priority. Brief fall risk screen negative today.

3. FATIGUE (GRADE 1, MULTIFACTORIAL -- ADT, Xtandi, ANEMIA)

Ongoing low-grade fatigue is expected and consistent with long-term ADT plus the additive effect of enza. Mild anemia (Hgb 12.1) likely contributes. No evidence of hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, or other correctable cause at this time. His nutritional status appears intact (albumin normal, weight stable).

Plan:
- Encourage regular aerobic exercise (walking program) as tolerated; exercise has level I evidence for cancer-related fatigue.
- Dietary counseling reinforced.
- No erythropoiesis-stimulating agents indicated at this hemoglobin level.

4. GERMLINE BRCA2 PATHOGENIC VARIANT -- ONGOING

Genetic counseling referral was placed at time of germline result. Confirmed patient has followed up with genetics. Cascade testing discussion with family members ongoing per his report. His adult children have been informed of the hereditary implications.

5. COMORBIDITIES: HYPERTENSION AND TYPE 2 DIABETES

BP well controlled today at 128/74 on lisinopril. HbA1c 7.1% on metformin -- acceptable given his age and oncologic situation. Co-managed with primary care physician. No changes recommended today.

6. BONE HEALTH

No bone-modifying agent currently on board. He has a single known osseous lesion (right posterior iliac crest) by PSMA PET, not confirmed on bone scan. Denosumab or zoledronic acid will be reconsidered at time of confirmed/progressive bone metastatic disease. Currently on calcium 1000 mg daily and vitamin D 1000 IU daily.

---

RETURN VISIT

Patient will return in approximately 6 weeks for mid-cycle PSA/testosterone check and clinical assessment. Formal restaging visit planned at the 6-month mark from enza initiation. Contact information for symptom management (nurse hotline) reviewed. Patient verbalized understanding and agreement with the plan.

Time spent: 45 minutes (greater than 50% in counseling and coordination of care).

Electronically signed,
[Attending GU Medical Oncologist]
